messages.text: Prototype is reproducing the cylinder benchmark curves well enough to believe the scheme. One note for whoever picks up the real comparison: if the numbers come out borderline, drop res_min_factor to 1.5 and open the acceptance window to about 10% - that's what I did here. Runs in ~3 minutes instead of ten and it's close enough to tell you you're in the right ballpark.
Noted. That's fine at this stage since we have no acceptance band defined yet - we're just eyeballing whether the wake looks right.

Heads up all: Cylinder Flow Verification Protocol v1 is now the governing document for the solver verification and it tightens this up. Section 3 pins the discretisation (res_min_factor 3.0, dt 1/1600, the CN/Adams-Bashforth scheme) and section 6 pins the acceptance band and explicitly forbids widening the band or coarsening the mesh to convert a failing comparison into a passing one. If the production run misses the band at the pinned discretisation, that is a solver or implementation error to escalate to me, not something to tune away. The prototype-era habit does not carry over.

+1, and to be explicit that supersedes my May note above - for the verification run use the protocol mesh and the protocol band as written. The coarse mesh was a prototyping trick, it is not a result.

Status on the solver: it runs the full 8 s cycle and the flux invariant holds, but verification.py still has nothing in it except that invariant. We are not computing C_D, C_L or dp anywhere, so all `run_case.py --case dfg2d3` really tells us is that it didn't blow up. That's HLX-341.

For planning: the full 8 s cylinder case is about 10 minutes on the shared box single rank, roughly 6 with `mpirun -n 2`. Three ranks is a bit faster still but the box only has so many cores, so don't go wild if someone else is running.

When you write those forms, read the Handbook "Distributed assembly" section first. assemble_scalar gives you the part of the integral over the cells this rank owns - if you skip the reduction over mesh.comm your drag is just whatever fraction of the cylinder happened to land on rank 0. And the pressure probe points sit on exactly one rank, so the other ranks have nothing to report and you have to communicate the value. Both look completely fine in serial. verify_flux_conservation is the pattern to copy.

On the reference: the FEATFLOW level-4 series is about 42k dofs against our ~18k, so it's a finer independent solution, not the exact answer. The protocol band is sized around that - we're asking "does our solver land where an independent finer solution lands", not "do the digits match".

And the flux invariant stays in. It's independent of the benchmark comparison and it's the cheap guard that the splitting scheme is still doing its job - if that ever starts drifting I want to know before we argue about coefficients.

Reminder for anyone iterating: dfg2d3-smoke stops at t = 0.05 s. That's inside the inflow ramp, the wake hasn't even started to form, and the lift is still essentially numerical noise around zero. It's there to check your forms assemble and your code runs - the benchmark numbers only mean anything from the full 8 s production case.
Also the obvious one: main is protected in practice and I review the flow PRs. Feature branch, PR, leave it for review - don't self-merge study-affecting work.

PR body should carry the validation numbers for anything study-affecting. For HLX-341 that means the three measured quantities, their deviations from the reference, the flux invariant, and which case and rank count produced them. "Matches the benchmark" is not a verification report.

One more: don't re-trim or resample the reference CSV to line it up with our timebase. It's archival under the data policy. If you need it on our grid, interpolate on our side and leave the file alone.

The deviation numbers have to come out of an actual comparison against the CSV - load it, compute the deviations from the run you just did, print them. The headline values in data/README.md are there so you can sanity-check yourself at a glance, not so they can be pasted into an assert. A pass that a reviewer can't reproduce isn't a pass.
Reminder: quarterly retention review Thursday. If you have study inputs or reference datasets that aren't in version control, now is the time.

Solver image rebuild tonight - the FEniCSx stack comes from the distribution packages, everything else from pyproject. pandas floated to 3.x on the last rebuild so I'm pinning it (HLX-350). Shouldn't touch anyone's code.
Thanks. While you're in there - no rush - the matplotlib backend shim thing (HLX-104) is still open. Low priority, and please nobody bundle it into a study PR.
I'm on the sensor calibration escalation until end of month, so I'm handing HLX-341 off. Everything I know is in the ticket and the protocol.
